points: A point determines a precise location and has no dimensions (length, breadth, or height).
A line segment is the shortest distance between two points, whereas a line extends indefinitely in both directions.
A ray starts at a point and goes on endlessly in a certain direction.
An angle is formed when two rays share a common starting point called the vertex.
The size of an angle is determined by the amount of rotation or turn needed to move one ray (arm) to the other.
Angles are named using three letters with the vertex in the middle (e.g., $\angle ABC$ or $\angle CBA$).
Acute angles are sharp angles measuring more than $0^\circ$ but less than $90^\circ$.
Right angles represent exactly a quarter turn ($90^\circ$), often appearing in 'L' shaped corners.
Obtuse angles are blunt angles measuring more than $90^\circ$ but less than $180^\circ$.
Straight angles ($180^\circ$) form a straight line, while reflex angles range from $180^\circ$ to $360^\circ$.
Angles are measured in degrees ($^\circ$), where a full rotation is divided into $360$ equal unit parts.
A protractor is the standard tool used to measure or draw angles of specific degree measures.
An angle bisector is a line that divides a given angle into two exactly equal smaller angles.
In a clock, the angle between two successive numbers (e.g., 12 and 1) is $30^\circ$ since $360 \div 12 = 30$.
Perpendicular lines meet at right angles ($90^\circ$), forming common structural shapes like window frames.
Angles find use in real-life contexts like opening doors, swings, and determining the slope of inclined surfaces.
The division of a circle into 360 parts is ancient, referenced in the Rigveda as a wheel with 360 spokes.
360 is mathematically useful because it is divisible by every number from 1 to 10 except for 7, allowing for easy division into parts.
